exemplar.title: To what extent is reward variability, influenced by social media, impacting the increased prevalence of unhealthy emotional dependence in teenage romantic relationships in the United States.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is reward variability, influenced by social media, impacting the increased prevalence of unhealthy emotional dependence in teenage romantic relationships in the United States.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated adequately with clear headings and repeated reference to key constructs.
Research question is explicitly stated and mirrored throughout the essay.
Structure (table of contents, headings) supports coherent navigation and focus.
weaknesses: Lacks operational definitions for key variables (âreward variabilityâ, âemotional dependenceâ).
Methodology is limited and ad-hoc with minimal evidence of informed source selection.
Geographical scope (US focus) is mentioned but not empirically justified or operationalised.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place communication and question formulation in the 3â4 band but methodology in the 1â2 band. The topic is adequately communicated and the RQ clearly stated, yet the lack of operational definitions and a systematic methodology constrain the performance. Averaging these strands yields a holistic score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Generally accurate integration of psychology terminology (e.g., âintermittent reinforcementâ, âsynaptic plasticityâ).
Sound understanding of basic neurobiological concepts and social-media behaviours.
Accurate definitions of constructs like âunhealthy emotional dependenceâ, âghostingâ, and âgaslightingâ.
weaknesses: Occasional imprecision and colloquial phrasing weaken consistency.
Mixing of scholarly and non-scholarly sources reduces academic rigour.
Several claims lack direct empirical citation and some concepts are introduced without definition.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology use and subject knowledge both sit in the 3â4 band: correct but not consistently precise, sound but not excellent. Given the mixture of strengths and areas needing more scholarly support, a mid-band score of 3 out of 6 reflects the overall quality.

strengths: Insightful links drawn between neurobiological mechanisms and specific online behaviours (e.g., breadcrumbing, ghosting).
Good breadth of relevant sources, with peer-reviewed articles underpinning key points.
Nuanced analysis in sections on identity formation and emotional distress, showing depth in parts of the argument.
weaknesses: Analysis remains somewhat one-sided, portraying social media predominantly as harmful.
Empirical evidence is seldom quantified and alternative explanations are not thoroughly evaluated.
Critical evaluation of source limitations is limited, and some conclusions lack robust interrogation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research quality and analysis depth) sit solidly in band 4â6, while discussion and evaluation are lower (1â3 band). The work demonstrates clear analytical effort and relevant research but falls short of comprehensive critical evaluation. A balanced mid-band score of 5 out of 12 appropriately represents this performance.

strengths: Comprehensive table of contents and clear section headings enhance structural coherence.
Overall structure meets standard extended essay conventions, aiding reader navigation.
weaknesses: Inconsistent pagination and formatting issues with long quotations/URLs.
Lack of a systematic referencing style; some layout conventions (APA/MLA) are not consistently applied.
Redundancies and abrupt paragraph endings detract from flow and readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure is clearly appropriate (band 3) but layout and formatting exhibit multiple inconsistencies (band 1â2). Overall, the presentation meets only some conventions, warranting a score of 2 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychology terminologyâe.g., âintermittent reinforcementâ, âsynaptic plasticityâ, âlong-term potentiationâ, âgaslightingââis generally used correctly and integrated into explanations.  Occasional imprecision (e.g., âdopamine itself is pleasurableâ) and colloquial phrasing reduce consistency, so usage is mostly accurate rather than consistently precise, aligning with the 3â4 band.

Basic layout elements (title page, word count, bibliography) are present, but pagination is inconsistent and several long quotations/URLs break formatting.  APA/MLA style is not applied systematically.  These issues indicate that some but not all formatting conventions are met, aligning with the 1â2 band.

The student demonstrates sound understanding of basic neurobiological concepts (dopamine pathways, VTA, nucleus accumbens), addiction theory and social-media behaviours such as ghosting and breadcrumbing.  Most claims are linked to external sources, and the bibliography lists a reasonable range of academic papers.  Nonetheless explanations sometimes lapse into journalistic statements, and mixing scholarly and non-scholarly references weakens academic rigour, placing the work solidly in the âgood but not excellentâ 3â4 band.

The essay is a literature-based review; no primary data or systematic secondary search strategy is described.  Source selection appears ad-hoc (popular websites such as NeuroLaunch alongside peer-reviewed articles) with minimal discussion of why each was chosen.  Consequently the methodology is limited in scope and shows little evidence of informed, purposeful selection, corresponding to the 1â2 band.

The topicâsocial-media reward variability and emotional dependence in U.S. teenage relationshipsâis introduced clearly in the title, reiterated in the opening paragraph and kept in sight throughout the body sections.  Headings, a table of contents and repeated reference to the key constructs (reward variability, dependence, teens) show adequate, though not sophisticated, communication of purpose.  However, explanatory precision is uneven: terms such as âunhealthy emotional dependenceâ are described more narratively than analytically, and geographical focus (United States) is mentioned but not operationalised.  Overall the clarity is satisfactory but not consistently sharp, corresponding to the middle band.

Around twenty sources are cited, several of which are peer-reviewed neuroscience or social-psychology articles, indicating a reasonable breadth of relevant research.  The discussion usually links citations to points being made, showing clearâthough not comprehensiveârelevance to the RQ.  Gaps remain (no empirical prevalence data for U.S. teens, limited critical comparison of studies); hence quality is âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ, fitting the 4â6 band at its lower edge.

The research question is explicitly stated on the cover page and mirrors the wording used in the essay, showing clear statement.  It narrows the study to âreward variability â¦ unhealthy emotional dependence â¦ teenage romantic relationships â¦ United Statesâ, yet remains conceptually broad (multiple psychological constructs, nationwide population, no time frame) and the essay does not operationalise variables or indicate how âextentâ will be measured.  Connection to the ensuing discussion is evident but only partially focused, fitting the 3â4 band.

The essay contains a contents table, numbered headings, introduction, background, thematic sections, conclusion and bibliographyâmeeting expected EE conventions.  Section ordering is coherent and aids reader navigation, so overall structure is clearly appropriate.

While the narrative is logical, the argument remains largely one-sided: social media is portrayed as harmful with little consideration of contrary evidence or moderating variables.  Evaluation of sources is minimal and limitations are noted only briefly in passing.  Consequently the discussion is basic and partially critical at best, matching the 1â3 band.

The essay moves beyond description by linking neurobiological mechanisms to specific online behaviours and by drawing analogies to gambling reinforcement schedules, showing clear efforts at analysis.  However, many assertions are generalized, empirical evidence is seldom quantified, and alternative explanations (e.g., developmental or sociocultural factors) are not weighed in depth.  Conclusions are usually supported but not thoroughly interrogated, situating the work in the lower portion of the 4â6 band.

annotations.comment: The title is clearly stated at the beginning, effectively communicating the study focus. This satisfies the requirement for including a clear investigation title.

The research question is explicit but lacks operational definitions for measurement of âreward variabilityâ and âunhealthy emotional dependence.â Consider specifying variables and metrics to tighten focus.
The word count is provided and falls within the prescribed limit, demonstrating adherence to formatting requirements.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well-structured, facilitating navigation through the essay components.
The introduction is descriptive but overly narrative. It would benefit from early citation of empirical studies to ground claims in evidence and enhance academic rigor.
The concept of âburnoutâ is introduced without definition or citation. Provide a clear operational definition and reference a source to support its usage.
The discussion of reward variability is generic. Enhance analysis by citing specific empirical studies or quantitative data illustrating this phenomenon in social media contexts.

The link between teenage identity development and reward variability is stated broadly. Focus the argument by articulating how specific aspects of identity formation interact with digital reward systems.
Although the US focus is mentioned, there is no empirical justification. Incorporate national statistics or studies specific to US teenagers to contextualize the geographical scope.
The dopamine release explanation relies on a non-academic source (Simply Psychology). Replace or supplement with peer-reviewed neuroscience research for greater credibility.
The claim linking social media engagement to self-esteem lacks direct citation. Support this assertion with specific studies on social mediaâs psychological effects.
The definition of unhealthy emotional dependence is clear, well-articulated, and accurately captures the psychological concept.
The paragraph ends abruptly. Ensure sentences are complete and review paragraph structure to maintain coherence and readability.
This section outlines social anxiety risks but lacks examples or statistics. Integrate case studies or survey data to support the analysis.
The analysis of online identity versus real-life self is nuanced and demonstrates depth in evaluating social mediaâs psychological impact.
The psychological impact of love bombing is discussed without empirical data. Add study results or statistics quantifying these effects.
The link between breadcrumbing and intermittent reinforcement is theoretically sound and well-argued.
This complex sentence could be simplified for readability. Consider breaking it into two sentences to improve flow.
The ghosting section accurately defines the behavior and reflects correct use of psychological terminology.
The analysis of ghostingâs impact on accountability and emotional distress is insightful and demonstrates critical evaluation.
The gaslighting discussion is detailed but would benefit from linking to neurological mechanisms previously described in the neurobiology section.
This paragraph effectively situates gaslighting within the digital context, demonstrating understanding of its social-media facilitation.
The clear heading for âNeurobiology in reward variabilityâ enhances structural coherence and guides the reader effectively.
The description of manipulative behaviors (love bombing, breadcrumbing, etc.) is accurate and demonstrates solid subject terminology use.
The love bombing discussion is descriptive but one-sided. Incorporate counterarguments or evidence of variability in genuine versus manipulative behaviors.
Repetitive phrasing reduces clarity. Vary sentence structure and eliminate redundancy for more concise writing.
The section reiterates previous points about reward variability. Consider consolidating to avoid redundancy and strengthen the focus.
The concluding remarks summarize the essay but do not propose specific strategies. Add actionable recommendations to enhance the conclusionâs impact.
The assertion âdopamine itself is pleasurableâ is simplistic. Clarify that dopamine signals reward prediction rather than pleasure itself, referencing current neuroscience literature.
This bridge between reward variability theory and teenage emotional dependence demonstrates a focused, coherent connection to the research question.
